meta.title: Lincoln Audit Explorer
meta.subtitle: A plain-language guide to what Lincoln's audits found, what improved, and what still needs attention.
meta.coverage: Public audit and City records organized in the public records for 2016–2025
meta.disclaimer: This public tool summarizes documented evidence from the public records. Interpretive statements are labeled and are not auditor conclusions.

questions.question: Can I trust the City's finances?
questions.shortAnswer: The evidence shows real improvement, but not every important weakness has been fully resolved.
questions.answer: Later audits stopped repeating several earlier findings, including weak separation of duties, missing expenditure approvals, budgeted-cash problems, parking-ticket controls, and some documentation issues. However, the latest completed audit still found that auditors had to propose accounting entries to prepare complete financial statements. That means the overall direction improved, but residents should still expect continued oversight.
questions.fact: Several findings were not repeated in the 2022–23 audit.
questions.interpretation: That is evidence of improvement, not proof that every control will continue working during staffing or system changes.

residentContext.concern: The audits found serious recordkeeping and financial-control problems. That is a reason to pay attention, but it is not the same as proof that money was stolen.
residentContext.meaning: The findings were mainly about how the City tracked, checked, approved, and reported public money. They did not automatically change taxes, utility rates, or City services.

questions.question: Why were there so many audit findings?
questions.shortAnswer: Many problems came from a small office handling too many financial duties without enough independent review or documentation.
questions.answer: The audit findings were not all separate problems. Several were connected: too few people handled collections, deposits, checks, accounting entries, and bank reconciliations; supporting records were incomplete; and year-end financial statements required auditor corrections. These weaknesses can reinforce one another.
questions.fact: Segregation of duties, audit adjustments, documentation, reconciliations, and approval evidence appeared across multiple audit cycles.
questions.interpretation: The pattern points to limited administrative capacity and weak control design more than one isolated mistake.

residentContext.concern: A long list of findings can look alarming. Many of the findings were connected to the same underlying problems, such as too few people handling too many financial duties and missing records.
residentContext.meaning: One weak process can create several findings. For example, poor recordkeeping can affect bank matching, approvals, financial statements, and utility billing at the same time.

questions.question: Has the City improved?
questions.shortAnswer: Yes. The latest completed audit did not repeat many earlier findings, but some high-risk areas remain.
questions.answer: The strongest evidence of improvement is that several recurring findings disappeared from the 2022–23 audit. Council records also show new policies, spending thresholds, contract review, competitive purchasing, ethics requirements, and a fraud-policy framework. The remaining question is whether these changes will work consistently over future audit cycles.
questions.fact: The public records classifies the overall trend as improving while noting residual risk.
questions.interpretation: A finding disappearing is meaningful, but one clean cycle does not guarantee permanent correction.
searchAliases: has the city fixed the problems

residentContext.concern: Improvement matters, but it does not mean every problem is solved. Residents should look at both what disappeared from later audits and what was still reported.
residentContext.meaning: The later audits reported fewer problems. That suggests progress in several areas, while some major issues still needed attention.

questions.question: What were the biggest problems?
questions.shortAnswer: The most serious recurring problems involved financial-statement preparation, fraud-risk planning, staff duty conflicts, and bank reconciliations.
questions.answer: The audit-adjustment finding and fraud-risk-assessment finding continued through every completed audit cycle in the public records. Other major weaknesses included incompatible duties, incomplete approval records, missing utility-billing support, and inaccurate or delayed reconciliations.
questions.fact: Audit adjustments and the missing fraud risk assessment recurred through the 2022–23 audit.
questions.interpretation: These issues matter because they affect whether financial information can be prepared accurately and independently.

residentContext.concern: The most serious issues involved missing support, money that could not be fully traced, financial statements that needed major corrections, and weak fraud-risk planning.
residentContext.meaning: These issues matter because they affect how confidently residents can rely on the City's financial records and how quickly mistakes can be found.

questions.question: What still needs attention?
questions.shortAnswer: Year-end financial reporting, fraud-risk follow-through, timely reconciliations, and controls that survive staff turnover.
questions.answer: The public records identifies unresolved questions about whether City staff can complete the annual close without material auditor entries, whether the fraud risk assessment was completed, whether monthly reconciliations are timely and independently reviewed, and whether new finance roles clearly separate incompatible duties.
questions.fact: Later council records show corrective activity, but the latest evidence does not establish that every new control is operating effectively.
questions.interpretation: The main risk is not a lack of activity; it is whether the changes are complete, documented, and durable.

residentContext.concern: Ongoing findings deserve attention because repeated problems can continue to create risk even when no theft or fraud has been proven.
residentContext.meaning: Residents may want to watch whether later audits confirm that the City's newer policies and procedures are working as intended.

questions.question: Was there fraud?
questions.shortAnswer: The audits identified fraud risk and control weaknesses, but the public records does not document an auditor finding that fraud occurred.
questions.answer: A fraud risk assessment is a structured review of where fraud could happen and how the City would prevent or detect it. Auditors repeatedly found that Lincoln had not completed that assessment. This is a serious control weakness, but it is not the same as finding that someone committed fraud.
questions.fact: The recurring finding was the absence of an organization-wide fraud risk assessment.
questions.interpretation: Weak controls can increase opportunity and reduce detection, but they do not by themselves prove theft or fraud.

residentContext.concern: The audits did not conclude that fraud occurred. They did report weaknesses that could make errors or misuse harder to prevent or detect.
residentContext.meaning: The key issue is whether the City has strong enough checks, records, and review procedures to protect public money and explain where it went.

questions.question: What does a qualified opinion mean?
questions.shortAnswer: It means auditors could verify most of the financial statements, but not one important part.
questions.answer: In Lincoln's earlier audits, missing utility-billing and collection records prevented auditors from testing parts of the enterprise funds. A qualified opinion does not mean every number was wrong. It means the auditors could not obtain enough evidence about a specific area to give a fully clean opinion on that part.
questions.fact: Missing or deleted utility support affected the audit opinion on enterprise-fund information.
questions.interpretation: The opinion was limited because evidence was unavailable, not because the audit proved all utility activity was improper.

residentContext.concern: A qualified opinion is important, but it does not mean the entire audit failed. It means auditors could not fully verify a specific part of the financial information.
residentContext.meaning: For residents, this means there was an important limit on how much confidence auditors could place in that part of the City's records.
residentContext.money: In this case, the issue involved utility operations. Utility money comes from water and sewer customers and is kept separate from other City money.

topics.title: Audit adjustments and year-end close
topics.plainTitle: Could the City prepare complete financial statements on its own?
topics.summary: Auditors had to propose entries needed to prepare complete and accurate financial statements in every completed audit cycle.
topics.whyItMatters: Residents and council members need financial statements that are complete, timely, and prepared through reliable City processes.
topics.whatImproved: Records show audit preparation, fund corrections, staffing changes, and increased review.
topics.whatRemains: The latest completed audit still repeated the finding, and later records show delayed or tabled financial statements and reconciliations.
topics.interpretation: Corrective work is visible, but effectiveness has not yet been established by a later completed audit.
evidence: Recurring Findings  — Audit adjustments / year-end close
2025 Council Evidence — deferred statements, reconciliation, and finance restructuring
timeline: Auditors proposed entries needed to complete the statements.
Still present in the latest completed audit.
Records show corrections, preparation work, and staffing changes, but also delays.

topics.title: Fraud risk assessment
topics.plainTitle: Did the City formally identify where fraud could happen?
topics.summary: Auditors repeatedly found that the City had not completed an organization-wide fraud risk assessment.
topics.whyItMatters: A formal assessment helps leaders identify opportunities for fraud and assign controls before a loss occurs.
topics.whatImproved: Council approved a fraud policy and assessment framework in late 2025. In January 2026, Council members reported continued work with the city office, and the mayor requested monthly audit-status updates.
topics.whatRemains: The public records reviewed do not include a completed fraud-risk assessment, formal conclusions, or a later independent audit confirming that the finding was resolved.
topics.fact: Material weakness in every completed audit cycle through 2022–23.
topics.interpretation: The policy is a meaningful first step, but a policy is not the same as a completed and operating risk assessment.
evidence: 2025 Council Evidence — December 18 fraud policy and assessment framework
2026 Council Evidence — January 8 and January 22 fraud-risk work and monthly audit-status reporting
timeline: Council approved a policy and participation plan; completion is not documented.
Council records document continued fraud-risk-assessment work and monthly audit-status reporting.

topics.title: Separation of financial duties
topics.plainTitle: Were too many financial duties handled by the same people?
topics.summary: Earlier audits found that too few people handled incompatible accounting duties.
topics.whyItMatters: Separating approval, recordkeeping, custody of money, and reconciliation makes errors or misuse easier to prevent and detect.
topics.whatImproved: The finding was not repeated in the 2022–23 audit after the City reported separating duties among four administrative staff.
topics.whatRemains: Later staffing transitions and proposed role changes make the durability of the control uncertain.
topics.fact: Material weakness through 2020–21; not repeated in 2022–23.
topics.interpretation: The audit outcome improved, but future audits must show the control survives vacancies and restructuring.
timeline: Too few people handled incompatible duties.
Final management and finance role design is not documented.

topics.title: Council approval of expenditures
topics.plainTitle: Was there proof that City spending was approved?
topics.summary: Earlier audits found missing evidence that some tested expenditures received Council approval.
topics.whyItMatters: Public spending should have clear support, correct coding, and approval by the proper authority.
topics.whatImproved: The finding was not repeated in the 2020–21 or 2022–23 audits. Later records show roll-call approvals, revised purchasing thresholds, legal review, and competitive purchasing.
topics.whatRemains: The public records cannot verify that every transaction file is complete or that every deferred payable was later approved.
topics.fact: 21 of 45 tested expenditures totaling $247,214 lacked approval evidence in 2016–17; a later cycle also repeated the problem.
topics.interpretation: Visible Council action supports improvement, but transaction-level completeness still requires audit testing.
evidence: Recurring Findings  — Council approval of expenditures
2025 Council Evidence — procurement thresholds and roll-call approvals
timeline: Approval evidence missing for 21 of 45 tested expenditures.
Approval evidence was again incomplete in tested items.
Revised thresholds and documented approvals appear in Council records.

topics.title: Utility billing and collection support
topics.plainTitle: Could auditors verify utility bills and deposits?
topics.summary: Missing or deleted utility records prevented auditors from testing whether billings and deposits were proper.
topics.whyItMatters: Utility records should show what customers were billed, what was collected, what changed, and what was deposited.
topics.whatImproved: The finding was not repeated in the 2022–23 audit. In February 2026, Council records documented work toward selecting a third-party collections agency for delinquent accounts.
topics.whatRemains: The 2026 minutes document planning and selection work, but they do not document implementation results or independently verify the current billing, collection, retention, backup, and reconciliation controls.
topics.fact: The 2016–17 audit cited $2,725,278 in collections that could not be fully tested because support was not retained.
topics.interpretation: The later audit outcome supports improvement, but the exact permanent control design remains unclear.
evidence: Recurring Findings  — Utility billing and collection support
2026 Council Evidence — February 19 delinquent-account collections process
timeline: Support was unavailable for utility and enterprise collections.
The problem continued and affected the audit opinion.
Council records document review of collection-agency quotes and a planned selection process.

topics.title: Estimated budgeted cash
topics.plainTitle: Did City budgets include required beginning and ending cash?
topics.summary: Earlier budgets omitted estimated cash amounts required for budget and tax-levy calculations.
topics.whyItMatters: Cash estimates help show how much money is already available and how much must be raised.
topics.whatImproved: The finding was not repeated in 2022–23. Later records show detailed budget work sessions and formal adoption steps.
topics.whatRemains: Direct verification still requires reviewing the adopted budget schedules by fund.
topics.fact: The finding affected budget years 2016–2021 and included material noncompliance.
topics.interpretation: The latest audit result is positive, but public meeting minutes alone cannot verify every required schedule.
evidence: 2025 Council Evidence — budget work sessions and adoption
timeline: Council records show line-item review, hearing, and adoption.

topics.title: Bank reconciliations
topics.plainTitle: Were bank records matched to the City's books accurately and on time?
topics.summary: Earlier audits found wrong-period checks, cleared checks left outstanding, and inaccurate cash balances.
topics.whyItMatters: Monthly reconciliation is a basic check that the bank and accounting records agree.
topics.whatImproved: The finding was not repeated in 2022–23.
topics.whatRemains: Public records from 2025 and 2026 show that timeliness still deserves monitoring. In March 2026, the February bank reconciliation was reported incomplete during an interim staffing transition. Later records show financial reports were again presented and approved, but do not state the exact reconciliation completion date.
topics.fact: The 2016–17 audit cited $203,758 in wrong-year checks and $32,708 in already-cleared checks.
topics.interpretation: The formal audit result improved, while later delays suggest the process still deserves close monitoring.
evidence: 2026 Council Evidence — March 19 incomplete February reconciliation and later financial-report follow-up
timeline: Cash was understated because of reconciliation errors.
Several financial items were delayed or tabled.
The February reconciliation was reported incomplete during the interim finance-staffing period.

topics.title: Supporting records and capital assets
topics.plainTitle: Did the City keep invoices, titles, and other proof?
topics.summary: Earlier audits found missing support for assets, vehicle titles, expenditures, and accounting adjustments.
topics.whyItMatters: Good records show what was purchased, who approved it, who owns it, and how it was recorded.
topics.whatImproved: The finding was not repeated in 2022–23. Later records show stronger purchasing, contract, disposition, and archiving practices.
topics.whatRemains: The public records cannot verify that every historical gap was resolved or every current transaction file is complete.
topics.fact: The 2016–17 audit cited missing support for $97,900 of additions and titles for $207,492 of vehicle additions.
topics.interpretation: Policies and visible procedures are stronger, but completeness is ultimately a transaction-level question.

overviewTimeline.summary: Auditors reported several major weaknesses in financial controls, records, approvals, and year-end reporting.
Several earlier findings appeared again, showing that important fixes were not yet complete.
Some findings disappeared, but major weaknesses in year-end reporting and fraud-risk planning remained.
Many older findings were not repeated. Two major findings still remained.
Council records show continued audit-related policy work, fraud-risk planning, utility-collection follow-up, staffing continuity efforts, and ongoing financial reporting.

auditOverview.summary: Auditors reported major weaknesses in controls, records, approvals, and year-end reporting.
Several earlier findings appeared again, showing that important fixes were not yet complete.
Some findings disappeared, while major reporting and fraud-risk weaknesses remained.
Many older findings were not repeated. This is the latest completed independent audit included here.

documentedUpdatesOverview.summary: City records show continued financial reporting work, policy activity, and follow-up after the latest audit.
Public records show purchasing changes, fraud-policy work, staffing changes, budget activity, and continued financial-process updates.
Council records document continued fraud-risk work, monthly audit-status reporting, utility-collection planning, and financial-report follow-up during a finance-office transition.

missing money.title: Looking for information about missing money?
missing money.answer: Auditors reported money-related records they could not fully verify. In the 2018–19 audit, three tested clerk-of-court fee items totaling $3,180 could not be traced to the City's general fund. In the 2020–21 audit, auditors identified $14,052 in court fees they could not trace. Auditors also could not test millions of dollars in utility collections because supporting records were missing or had been deleted. These findings do not prove theft, but they do mean the available records did not fully account for or verify certain money.

stolen money.title: Looking for information about stolen money?
stolen money.answer: The audits did not conclude that theft occurred. They did report court-fee amounts that could not be traced to the general fund and utility collections that could not be tested because records were unavailable. That is a serious accountability problem, but it is not the same as an auditor finding that someone stole the money.

corruption.title: Looking for information about corruption?
corruption.answer: The records summarized here do not include an auditor conclusion that corruption occurred. They do include money-related amounts that could not be traced, collections that could not be tested, and repeated weaknesses in fraud-risk planning and financial controls.

failed audit.title: Looking for information about a failed audit?
failed audit.answer: Audits are not simply passed or failed. Earlier audits included a qualified opinion in a specific area because supporting records were unavailable.

audits.cardSummary: Auditors reported 10 problems involving financial records, approvals, utility billing, bank records, and other basic safeguards.
audits.oneSentence: Auditors found several serious weaknesses in how the City tracked money, approved spending, kept records, and prepared financial information.
audits.opinionPlain: Auditors could not give a fully clean opinion on some City-run utility operations because important billing and collection records were missing.
audits.whyItMattered: When records are missing or financial duties are not checked by another person, mistakes can be harder to find and public money can be harder to track.
keyPoints: Too few people handled too many financial duties.
The City could not show Council approval for 21 of 45 tested expenses.
Auditors had to suggest entries needed to complete the financial statements.
Utility records were missing, so auditors could not fully test $2.7 million in collections.
Bank-record errors caused the City's cash balance to be understated.
audits.whatChangedNext: Many of these problems were reported again in the next audit, showing that the fixes were not yet complete.

periods.label: During the 2016–17 audit period
periods.note: A complete council list for this period is still being verified from archived meeting minutes.

audits.cardSummary: Auditors again reported 10 problems. Missing records and bank differences made parts of the City's finances impossible to fully verify.
audits.oneSentence: Many earlier problems continued, and auditors could not give an opinion on the City's 2019 financial statements because reliable evidence was not available.
audits.opinionPlain: For 2019, auditors said they did not have enough reliable information to express an opinion on the financial statements.
audits.whyItMattered: The City was approving bills and receiving reports, but the underlying records still did not fully match or provide enough proof for the auditors.
keyPoints: Most of the problems from the earlier audit were reported again.
Utility billing records were deleted because the software ran out of storage.
Three tested court-fee items totaling $3,180 could not be traced to the City's general fund.
The year-end bank records did not match the City's accounting records.
Even after large corrections, a $118,634 difference remained.
audits.whatChangedNext: The next audit showed some improvement in the audit opinion, but several financial-control problems continued.

periods.label: During the 2018–19 audit period
periods.note: A complete council list for this period is still being verified from archived meeting minutes.

audits.cardSummary: Auditors reported 8 problems. Some parts of the audit improved, while missing support and money that could not be traced remained concerns.
audits.oneSentence: The audit showed partial improvement, but the City still had serious problems with records, financial checks, court fees, and bank matching.
audits.opinionPlain: Auditors gave a clean opinion on the main government finances, but not on several utility-related funds because some older accounting changes could not be verified.
audits.whyItMattered: Some records were improving, but auditors still could not confirm important information needed to fully trust every part of the financial statements.
keyPoints: Too few staff still handled duties that should have been divided among different people.
Auditors again had to suggest entries needed to complete the financial statements.
Important invoices, asset records, and accounting support were missing.
Auditors could not trace $14,052 in court fees to the general fund.
The City's accounting records were $208,089 higher than the reconciled bank cash.
audits.whatChangedNext: By the 2022–23 audit, several long-running problems were no longer reported as findings.

periods.label: Council documented in December 2020 minutes
periods.note: Council membership may have changed during the two-year audit period. These names are taken from a documented public meeting.

audits.cardSummary: Auditors reported 6 problems. Several older problems disappeared, while new issues involved fund transfers, debt rules, conflicts, and accounting entries.
audits.oneSentence: The latest completed audit showed real improvement, but it also found six problems that still needed attention.
audits.opinionPlain: Auditors gave clean opinions on the financial statements, meaning they had enough evidence and found the statements fairly presented under the City's accounting method.
audits.whyItMattered: A cleaner audit opinion was an important improvement, but it did not mean every financial rule or safety check was working correctly.
keyPoints: Several earlier findings were no longer reported.
Utility-fund transfers exceeded the legal limit by $252,181.
The City did not follow two requirements tied to a $1.858 million water bond.
Auditors still had to suggest entries needed to complete the financial statements.
The City still had not completed a full fraud-risk review.
Some bank-transfer entries were recorded incorrectly and required major corrections.
audits.whatChangedNext: Public records from 2024 and 2025 show continued policy, staffing, purchasing, budgeting, and financial-process work after this audit.

periods.label: Early 2022
periods.note: Public meeting records show changes in council membership during 2022.

periods.label: Late 2022 and early 2023
periods.mayor: Gerald Wise through December 2022; Carrie Praska served as Interim Mayor in early 2023
periods.note: A special election was scheduled in 2023, so leadership changed during this audit period.
